# Transcrição: 02Story- The Spoon of Power

- **Fonte:** 02Story- The Spoon of Power
- **Duração:** 08:15
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

I'm standing in front of an enormous pile of leaves on the playground behind my school. It's moving this pile of leaves because somewhere in there is my fifth grade student, Jamie. His hand emerges from the leaves and in it there's a silver object. He shouts, look, a spoon. And I look and he's right. It's a spoon. It's a kitchen spoon. It probably fell out of a lunchbox yesterday or 5,000 years ago. He says, look what I found. It's a spoon. And I tell Jamie, no, it's not a spoon. It's the spoon of power. And somehow Jamie knows what this means. Jamie knows that by declaring it the spoon of power, I must now have it. And I know he knows this because as soon as I say it, he is off and running and I am chasing. And for the next 17 minutes, my responsibilities as an elementary school teacher are gone. The 75 kids that are playing on the playground, nothing matters to me anymore except Jamie and that spoon. And I chase them across a field and over the play scape, we leap over swings. We run through the soccer field. 17 minutes later, the bell rings and Jamie still has the spoon. And I can't believe it because I genuinely tried to catch that little kid and rip that spoon from his hands. But it's OK because Jamie's in my class. There's two hours left in the school day and he's only 10 years old. And I know that 10-year-old boys have the attention span of a goldfish. He thinks he'll pay attention to the spoon for the rest of the day, but I know better. And so we're in the classroom and we're doing a math lesson. I'm doing math on the board and Jamie has put the spoon on the corner of his desk like he's taunting me. I'm doing a multiplication problem on the board. I've got one eye on the board and one eye on the spoon. And Jamie's got one eye in his notebook and one eye on the spoon and we're going back and forth and back and forth. And then I'm reading a book to the kids. I'm sitting on a stool. I've got one eye on my book and one eye on the spoon. And Jamie's got one eye on me and one eye on the spoon. And I give him credit. He's focused for a long time. But then it's time to write something Jamie loves. And so he takes out his journal and he starts writing. And as he's writing, I watch his head slowly fall down to rest in his arm. He's writing and he's lost in his story. And now I know it's time for me to get that spoon. And so from the back of the room, I creep down the center aisle very quietly, very slowly. I get right up to Jamie. I reach over to him and the spoon is gone. He turns to me and he says, did you really think I was just going to leave it there for you to take? I can't believe it. I turn to the girl sitting next to him and I say, where's the spoon? And she says, I don't know what you're talking about. I turn to the boy, where is the spoon? I don't know what you're talking about. I threaten years of homework if they do not tell me where the spoon is.

## [03:02 - 06:02]

And as I'm having my fit, the bell rings. The school day has ended. Jamie leaps from his seat. He runs to his locker. He grabs his coat. He heads towards the door and he stops at the library. He pulls out the S-Box of books. And from the S-Box, he pulls out the spoon. He says, I filed it under S and he's out the door. He has stolen the spoon of power. I am never going to see it again. I cannot believe it. The next school day, Jamie walks into the classroom and he has the spoon of power on a chain around his neck. And I say, how did you? And he says, my father drilled the hole and my mother gave me the chain. He's wearing it on his neck and as terrible of a person as I am, I know I can't really rip it from his neck. So now he just walks through the class taunting me all day with the spoon of power, laughing at me for not getting it from him. I can't believe it. Two days later, it's time to take our math test and Mackenzie is losing her mind, as she always does before every math test. Because the prospect of getting a 99% rather than a 100% on a math test is too much for Mackenzie to handle. So every Friday, I have to calm her down, assure her that everything's going to be fine. And as I'm doing this, Jamie stands up and he walks over to Mackenzie. He takes the spoon off his neck and puts it around Mackenzie's neck and says, this will help you. And it does. Mackenzie has never been more calm while taking a math test in her life. Three days later, David's grandfather dies. And he's out of school for two days. And when David returns on the third day, Jamie is by the door waiting for David. He drops that spoon around David's neck and says, I think you need this today. And he does. Throughout the rest of that school year, every time a kid forgets their homework and has to face me, every time they're being bullied on the playground, every time they're having a problem on the bus, Jamie takes the spoon of power, puts it around their neck and changes their day in a wonderful way. On the very last day of school, I gather my class together for the final hour. I teach elementary school, the very last year of elementary school, which means once the bell rings, these kids are off for the summer and many of them are going to go off to different schools and not see each other anymore. It's our last chance to be together as a family. And so I always ask the kids, does anyone have something they'd like to say? Jamie stands up and he walks to the front of the room. He takes the spoon off his neck and he tries to hand it to me. And I say, no, Jamie. I say, there was a day when I legitimately tried to get that spoon from you, but it's yours now. You've proven that you should be the person who has the spoon of power.

## [06:02 - 08:15]

But Jamie says, no. Jamie says, the power of the spoon only works in my classroom. He tells me that it's my job to take the spoon and next year, when someone needs it, it's my job to put it around their neck. And then he pulls a chair over to me and he stands on it so he can get eye to eye with me and he takes the spoon off his neck and he puts it on mine. Last year was the hardest year I have taught in my life through the course of the pandemic. It was also one of the best years I have ever taught in my life. And throughout last year, I felt like I was the luckiest teacher in America because I had the spoon of power. 15 years later, I still have the spoon of power. And last year, I used this spoon more than I've ever used it before. When a child's parents were sick with COVID or a colleague got sick, when someone lost a loved one, I used this spoon constantly. And every single time, Jamie's spoon did more for that student than anything I could. Jamie found a simple kitchen spoon in a pile of leaves a long time ago. And somehow through the magic of a 10-year-old boy, he made it something more than just a spoon. And so truly, I feel like the luckiest teacher in the world. I'm the only teacher who has a spoon that I can place around a student's neck and make their day better. It is the greatest gift I have ever been given as a teacher. It is my most powerful teaching tool. And I have a little boy named Jamie Calvert to thank for it. Thank you.
